chunk_summary: Deworming in SAM management is a rehabilitation-phase intervention initiated after clinical stabilization, focusing on age-appropriate anthelmintic dosing and hygiene counseling to prevent reinfection.
learning_objectives: Understand the timing and rationale for deworming in severe acute malnutrition (SAM) management.
Learn age-specific anthelmintic dosing protocols for albendazole and mebendazole.
Recognize contraindications and adverse effects of deworming in SAM patients.
Integrate deworming with hygiene education to reduce reinfection risks.
key_facts: Deworming is not a day-0 priority in SAM management; it is initiated after 48–72 hours or at discharge.
Albendazole dosing: 200 mg single dose for 12–23 months, 400 mg single dose for ≥24 months.
Mebendazole alternative: 100 mg twice daily for 3 days in children ≥24 months.
Avoid deworming in children <12 months unless directed by specific program policies.
Persistent diarrhea requires stool examination for pathogens rather than empiric deworming.
Link deworming to outpatient follow-up with hygiene counseling to reduce reinfection.
reference_quote: Deworming should be planned once the child is clinically stable, has transitioned to catch-up feeding, and can tolerate oral medicines reliably (commonly after the first 48–72 hours of stabilization or at discharge).
document_key_topics: Integration of hygiene counseling with deworming
Contraindications and adverse effects of deworming
